title: Overfitting
description: When a model fits training noise so closely that held-out or production data perform worse—the classic gap between memorization and useful generalization.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: Overfitting is poor generalization caused by excessive fit to the training distribution: the model minimizes training loss by memorizing examples instead of learning stable features. High capacity, noisy labels, and too many training steps relative to data size are common drivers.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Overfitting is the mirror of generalization: product quality depends on held-out performance, not training curves alone. Linking overfitting to model capacity, loss design, and alignment data hygiene keeps architecture, training, and evaluation vocabulary consistent.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: A classifier reaches 99% training accuracy but 60% on a validation set because it memorized background textures in training photos. Adding augmentation, early stopping, or weight decay widens the train–validation gap in the right direction.

whereItAppears.title: Where It Appears
whereItAppears.body: Fine-tuning small datasets on large language models, tabular models with leaky features, and diffusion models that reproduce training captions verbatim. Loss-function and optimizer-state concepts describe the training machinery that must be regularized to reduce overfitting.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: Overfitting is not the same as underfitting or pure distribution shift: shift can hurt test performance even without memorization. Low training loss alone is not proof of overfitting—you need a comparison set. Memorizing public benchmarks is a form of overfitting to evaluation, not just training rows.

openingSummary: Overfitting means the model memorized spurious training patterns that fail on validation or live data, often visible when validation loss rises while training loss keeps falling.
